title: 👑 Elite challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: master of the Edinburgh journey

questions.prompt: Complete: "My brother ___ packed his bag, but my sisters ___ not finished yet."
questions.explanation: "My brother" is one person (he) → "has packed"; "my sisters" is plural (they) → "have not finished". So: has … have ✓. The trap is using the same auxiliary for both — each subject decides its own.

questions.prompt: Complete: "We ___ from Tunis to Edinburgh in about four hours." (verb: fly)
questions.explanation: "We" takes "have", and the irregular participle of fly is "flown": have flown ✓. "Flew" is the simple past, "flied" invents a regular ending, and "has" does not agree with we.

questions.prompt: Only ONE sentence is correct. Which one?
questions.explanation: "You" takes "have", and the participle "visited" completes the question: Have you ever visited…? ✓. "Has" is wrong with you, "visit" is the base form, and "Did … visited" mixes the simple past auxiliary with a participle.

questions.prompt: Which statement about the present perfect is TRUE?

options.text: It is formed with 'have' or 'has' + the base form of the verb.
'Has' is used with I, you, we and they.
It is formed with 'have' or 'has' + the past participle.

questions.explanation: The present perfect = have/has + past participle ✓. Option a is wrong (it uses the participle, not the base form), b is wrong ("has" is for he/she/it, not I/you/we/they), and d is wrong (the participle of go is "gone", not "goed").

questions.prompt: Complete: "'___ you ever ___ in a Scottish castle?' the guide asked the ___."
questions.explanation: "You" takes "have", the regular participle of stay is "stayed", and the guide speaks to more than one visitor → "tourists": Have you ever stayed … tourists ✓. "Has" is wrong with you, "stay" is the base form, and "staid" is a wrong spelling of the participle.

questions.prompt: Complete: "'I have ___ my boarding pass!' 'Don't worry, you can print it at the ___.'" (verb: lose)
questions.explanation: The irregular participle of lose is "lost": I have lost ✓, and you print a boarding pass at the "check-in machine" at the airport. "Lose" is the base form, "losed" invents a regular ending, and a swimming pool is not where you print a boarding pass.
